evals.prompt: We're weighing whether to break our Rails monolith into microservices over the next two quarters. The team is split and people keep talking past each other — facts, fears, and hopes all jumbled together. Should we do it? Give me a thorough, balanced review of the decision.
evals.expected_output: A parallel six-hats review of the migration decision: the idea framed up front, six clearly-labelled single-mode perspectives, and a blue-hat synthesis with an explicit go/no-go-style conclusion and next steps.
expectations: Frames the specific decision under review (the monolith-to-microservices migration) before presenting the perspectives
Presents all six thinking hats, each clearly labelled by its colour or name (white, red, black, yellow, green, blue)
The red-hat contribution gives gut/emotional reactions and does NOT justify them with reasons or logic (no 'because' arguments)
The white-hat contribution separates established facts from assumptions/unknowns and names information that is missing
Keeps the black-hat (logically-reasoned risks) and yellow-hat (logically-reasoned benefits) as separate single-mode sections rather than a blended pros-and-cons list
The green-hat contribution proposes alternatives or concrete ways to overcome difficulties the black hat raised
The blue-hat synthesis ends with an explicit verdict (go / no-go / go-with-conditions / needs-more-info) plus concrete next steps

evals.prompt: Here's my plan: kill our free tier and move all new signups to a 14-day trial, to push conversion. Stress-test this for me — what are the angles I'm missing?
evals.expected_output: A six-hats stress-test that surfaces risks, the genuinely unknown information, and creative alternatives, not just a single opinion — closing with a synthesised recommendation.
expectations: Presents all six thinking hats, each clearly labelled by its colour or name
The white-hat contribution surfaces open questions and information that would be needed to decide (e.g. current free-to-paid conversion, free-tier acquisition role), not just opinions
The black-hat contribution raises logically-reasoned risks of removing the free tier (not mere dislike)
The green-hat contribution offers alternatives or modifications to the plan rather than only judging it
The red-hat contribution is expressed as unjustified gut reaction, kept separate from the reasoned hats
The blue-hat synthesis lays out where the perspectives agree and conflict and ends with a clear recommendation and next steps

evals.prompt: I'm incredibly excited about this — I think we should acquire our smaller competitor before year-end, and honestly I've half made up my mind already. Run this past the six thinking hats.
evals.expected_output: A balanced panel that surfaces the excitement openly (red) yet still applies neutral facts and genuine critical caution, so an enthusiasm-driven decision is judged cleanly rather than rubber-stamped.
expectations: Presents all six thinking hats, each clearly labelled by its colour or name
The red-hat contribution captures the stated excitement/enthusiasm and is expressed without justification
The black-hat contribution still raises genuine, logically-reasoned risks of the acquisition despite the user's stated enthusiasm
The white-hat contribution distinguishes known facts from assumptions and flags missing information (e.g. due-diligence data, integration cost)
The blue-hat synthesis gives an honest conclusion that does not simply endorse the pre-formed preference, and names the key trade-off or missing information
Does not collapse into a single blended argument — the perspectives remain separable

evals.prompt: Should we rewrite our notifications service in Go, or keep maintaining it in Node? Give me a 360-degree review before we commit either way.
evals.expected_output: A six-hats review of a forced either/or choice, ending in a blue-hat synthesis that resolves the fork (or flags it as a genuine one) rather than averaging the two options.
expectations: Frames the specific either/or choice (rewrite in Go vs keep in Node) before presenting the perspectives
Presents all six thinking hats, each clearly labelled by its colour or name
The yellow-hat contribution gives a reasoned positive case (logical benefits), distinct from raw enthusiasm
The white-hat contribution separates facts from assumptions and identifies missing information needed to choose
The green-hat contribution offers options beyond the binary (e.g. partial rewrite, incremental migration) or ways past the difficulties raised

The blue-hat synthesis reaches a clear decision on the fork — recommending an option with conditions, or explicitly flagging it as a genuine fork pending specific information — rather than averaging the two into mush
